questions.question: Explain the concept of Memory Hierarchy and its importance in computer system design.
keyPoints: Memory hierarchy organizes memory into levels based on speed, size, and cost
Levels: Registers → Cache → RAM → Secondary Storage
Faster memory is more expensive, so hierarchy balances performance and cost
Uses locality of reference principle to improve performance
Reduces average memory access time significantly

questions.question: Differentiate between SRAM and DRAM with respect to their construction, operation, and applications.
keyPoints: SRAM uses flip-flops, DRAM uses capacitors
SRAM is faster, DRAM is slower
SRAM does not need refresh, DRAM needs periodic refresh
SRAM is more expensive per bit, DRAM is cheaper
SRAM used for cache, DRAM used for main memory

questions.question: What is Memory Interleaving? Explain its advantages in memory organization.
keyPoints: Memory interleaving distributes consecutive addresses across multiple banks
Enables parallel access to reduce memory latency
Increases effective memory bandwidth
Common types: low-order and high-order interleaving
Reduces memory bottleneck in fast processors

questions.question: Describe the different types of Read-Only Memory (ROM) and their characteristics.
keyPoints: PROM: Programmable once after manufacturing
EPROM: Erasable using ultraviolet light, can be reprogrammed
EEPROM: Electrically erasable, can be erased and programmed in-circuit
Flash ROM: Block-based erasing, widely used in embedded systems
All are non-volatile, retain data without power

questions.question: Explain Big-Endian and Little-Endian byte ordering with an example.
keyPoints: Big-Endian: MSB stored at lowest address (like reading left to right)
Little-Endian: LSB stored at lowest address (like reading right to left)
Example: 0x12345678 at address 100: Big-Endian stores 0x12 at 100
Little-Endian stores 0x78 at 100
Important for data exchange between different systems

questions.question: What are the factors that affect memory performance? Explain each briefly.
keyPoints: Access Time: Time to read/write a memory location
Cycle Time: Minimum time between successive operations
Bandwidth: Amount of data transferred per unit time
Latency: Delay between request and data delivery
Data bus width affects throughput

questions.question: Explain the organization of main memory in a computer system. Discuss address decoding, bus interfacing, and factors determining memory capacity.
keyPoints: Main memory organization includes address bus, data bus, and control signals
Address decoding: converts binary address to chip select signals
Types: Linear decoding (simple) and Decoded decoding (efficient for large systems)
Memory capacity = 2^n × m bits where n=address bits, m=word size
Bus interfacing involves timing, wait states, and bus arbitration
Memory module organization includes banks, interleaving, and error correction

questions.question: Draw and explain the block diagram of memory organization. Discuss the role of memory controller and address mapping techniques.
keyPoints: Block diagram: CPU → Address Bus → Memory Controller → Memory Chips → Data Bus
Memory controller manages read/write operations, refresh cycles, timing
Address mapping techniques: Single chip, Linear, Partitioned decoding
Cache mapping: Direct, Fully Associative, Set-Associative
Virtual memory mapping uses page tables and TLB
Proper mapping reduces access time and improves efficiency

questions.question: A computer has 32-bit address bus and 32-bit data bus. Calculate: (a) Maximum memory capacity (b) Word size (c) If memory is organized in 8-bit bytes, how many address lines are needed for byte addressing?
keyPoints: (a) With 32-bit address 2^32 addresses = 4 GB: capacity
(b) Word size equals data bus width = 32 bits (4 bytes)
(c) For byte addressing with 32-bit data bus: 2^32 bytes = 4 GB
Each byte needs unique address, so full 32 address lines needed
Physical memory may be organized as 4 banks for 32-bit access
Effective addressable units = 2^32 bytes or 2^30 words

questions.question: Explain the difference between various memory mapping techniques used in cache organization. Which one provides the best hit ratio and why?
keyPoints: Direct Mapping: Each block maps to one cache line (simple, moderate hit ratio)
Fully Associative: Any block can go anywhere (best hit ratio, complex hardware)
Set-Associative: Compromise between above two (good hit ratio, practical)
Trade-off between hardware complexity, cost, and performance
Fully Associative gives best hit ratio but needs expensive comparator logic
Set-Associative (e.g., 8-way) offers near-associative performance at lower cost
